chunk_summary: This section discusses MUAC as a screening tool for malnutrition, emphasizing its validated use in children aged 6–59 months and emerging guidance for infants under six months, including new WHO criteria for identifying at-risk infants.
learning_objectives: Understand the validated use of MUAC for malnutrition screening in children aged 6–59 months.
Learn the emerging WHO guidance for identifying malnutrition risk in infants under six months.
Recognize the importance of pairing infant MUAC with clinical assessments and local protocols.
Explore the criteria for identifying infants at risk of poor growth and development.
key_facts: MUAC <115 mm is a standard criterion for severe acute malnutrition in children aged 6–59 months.
Infant MUAC is not equivalently validated for under six months but can be used as a pragmatic trigger for further assessment.
WHO 2023 guideline introduces an 'at risk of poor growth and development' framework for infants under six months.
Entry criteria for infants under six months include low weight-for-age, low weight-for-length, nutritional oedema, poor growth on sequential weights, and low infant MUAC.
Clinical assessment of feeding effectiveness, hydration, infection, and oedema is essential for infants under six months with low MUAC.
reference_quote: The 2023 WHO wasting guideline includes an 'at risk of poor growth and development' framework for infants and lists several possible entry criteria (for example low weight-for-age, low weight-for-length, nutritional oedema, poor growth on sequential weights, and low infant MUAC for the 6 weeks to <6 months age band).
document_key_topics: MUAC screening tool and age-specific limitations
Malnutrition criteria for infants under six months
WHO 2023 guidelines on wasting and nutritional oedema
Clinical assessment protocols for malnutrition in infants
